trajectory_summary: Clean, efficient call that completes all required beats and books the appointment without scope creep. The trajectory is functionally sound but energy-flat — Tyke never converts Enrique's self-articulated bill pain into emotional alignment against the utility, and the prior-design skepticism is noted but not resolved. The appointment is booked but the closer inherits an unconvinced customer with no context on what failed before.
outcome_dispute: The call books an appointment, so the primary outcome is a sit. However, the 'stagnant' shape reflects that Enrique's energy at the end of the call is essentially identical to his energy at the start — transactional and cooperative but not curious or excited. The appointment is structurally sound but emotionally thin.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: And then it looks like I have you down as the property owner. Is there anybody else with title with you, or is it just My wife. Your wife.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Tyke correctly identifies the wife as co-title holder and then books the appointment for Enrique alone. The schema's spouse_decision protocol is unambiguous: co-title holders must be present for the close. This is the highest-risk gap on the call — a closer who shows up to a design review with one of two decision-makers is structurally set up to hear 'I need to talk to my wife.' One sentence at the time-selection moment fixes this entirely.

key_moments.quote: Hi. Is this Enrique? Yes. Who is this? Yeah. Hey, Enrique. This is, Tyke with Ion. I was giving you a call because I received a request for a better debt solar for the home.
key_moments.stewart_read: The intro is garbled — 'a request for a better debt solar' is not intelligible as a legitimacy anchor. The schema's standard intro requires a clear reference to the form submission as a success signal. Tyke's phrasing fails that test. The customer's immediate 'Who is this?' suggests the intro did not land cleanly. This is a minor gap — Enrique recovers quickly and the call proceeds — but a clean intro would have eliminated the 'Who is this?' friction entirely.

key_moments.quote: I don't know. It didn't convince me.
key_moments.stewart_read: Tyke captures that Enrique has seen prior designs and that they failed to convince him, but never asks what company or what specifically fell short. The schema's prior_designs verify item explicitly exists to surface competitive context. Tyke gets the timeline (two to three years ago) but not the company or the failure mode. This is a reactive miss — there is no evidence Tyke is deferring this to the closer strategically; he simply moves on.

key_moments.quote: So I'm I'm wondering if maybe this time there's better kind of solar panels or maybe the price has go cheaper or something like that, and it's a little more affordable than it was.
key_moments.stewart_read: Enrique is doing the curiosity-creation work himself — he's already wondering if this time will be different, already generating hypotheses about improved panels and lower prices. This is a high-value signal: a customer who self-generates reasons to re-engage is primed for the closer's design reveal. Tyke responds by asking about timeline rather than feeding the curiosity. A suggested rephrase (not verbatim): 'That's exactly why it's worth taking another look — the design will show you what's changed for your specific home.' The hook is there; Tyke doesn't take it.

key_moments.quote: In order to qualify for the current programs, all we ask of the homeowner is you just have a credit score of at least 670. Is that gonna be an issue for you at all?
key_moments.stewart_read: Tyke inserts the credit qualifier during the natural dead air of Enrique navigating the Dominion website. This is a competent efficiency move — a required beat completed without burning additional call time. The threshold stated (670) matches the schema. The framing ('is that gonna be an issue for you at all?') is appropriately low-pressure. No coaching gap here on the qualifier itself.
